1.pericope_label: Taste and See That the Lord Is Good
1.synthesis: Psalm 34 is an acrostic thanksgiving psalm whose testimony and instruction interweave in nearly every verse. The opening vow is total and persistent: “I will bless the Lord at all times; his praise shall continually be in my mouth. My soul makes its boast in the Lord; let the humble hear and be glad” (Ps 34:1–2). The psalmist invites corporate rejoicing: “Oh, magnify the Lord with me, and let us exalt his name together! I sought the Lord, and he answered me and delivered me from all my fears” (34:3–4). The great invitation follows: “Oh, taste and see that the Lord is good! Blessed is the man who takes refuge in him!” (34:8). The angel of the Lord encamps around those who fear him and delivers them (34:7). Practical instruction in fear-of-the-Lord follows: “Come, O children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the Lord. What man is there who desires life and loves many days, that he may see good? Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from speaking deceit. Turn away from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it” (34:11–14). YHWH’s eyes are on the righteous; his face is against those who do evil (34:15–16). “The righteous cry out, and the Lord hears and delivers them out of all their troubles. The Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit” (34:17–18). Even many afflictions of the righteous will not ultimately prevail: “Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but the Lord delivers him out of them all” (34:19).
1.historical_context: The superscription connects Psalm 34 to David’s feigned madness before Abimelech (called Achish in 1 Sam 21:13–15)—a moment of deception that led to deliverance, suggesting the psalm reflects on the complexity of rescue through morally imperfect means. Like Psalm 25, Psalm 34 is an acrostic, using the alphabetical form to signal comprehensiveness: the full range of the fear of the Lord from aleph to taw. The invitation to “taste and see” (34:8) deploys a sensory metaphor unusual in the Psalter: the goodness of YHWH is something that can be empirically experienced, not merely asserted. Peter quotes this verse in the context of the church’s formation: “like newborn infants, long for the pure spiritual milk, that by it you may grow up into salvation—if indeed you have tasted that the Lord is good” (1 Pet 2:2–3). The instruction of 34:12–14 is quoted nearly verbatim in 1 Pet 3:10–12 as a guide for Christian conduct under suffering.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 34 alternates between testimony and wisdom-instruction in a way that makes the two indistinguishable: the psalmist’s personal experience of deliverance becomes the content of his teaching. The movement is outward: from boast (v. 2) to invitation (v. 3) to testimony (vv. 4–7) to the universal invitation to “taste and see” (v. 8) to direct instruction of “children” (vv. 11–14) to theological declaration about YHWH’s care for the righteous (vv. 15–22). The closing verse—“the Lord redeems the life of his servants; none of those who take refuge in him will be condemned” (34:22)—is the acrostic’s last stroke and the psalm’s final assurance: refuge in YHWH is not a naïve hope but a guaranteed outcome.
1.theological_application: “The Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit” (34:18) stands as one of the Psalter’s most direct consolations for those in suffering’s depths. It does not promise the absence of brokenness but the presence of YHWH within it—a proximity that the crushed spirit can access precisely because the crushing has reduced all self-sufficiency. Communities shaped by Psalm 34 learn the practice of “tasting and seeing” as a deliberate act of attention: paying notice to the ways in which the goodness of the Lord has been experienced in one’s own life, so that testimony can be given and the humble can hear and be glad (34:2). The transmission of faith from experienced person to “children” (34:11) is not merely catechetical instruction but the passing on of tested knowledge.
1.christology: “He keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken” (34:20) is cited in John 19:36 as fulfilled in the crucifixion: the soldiers did not break Jesus’ legs because he was already dead, fulfilling the Passover lamb prescription (Exod 12:46) and this psalm. Christ is the righteous one whose bones are guarded even in death. The invitation to “taste and see that the Lord is good” (34:8) receives its eucharistic register in the bread-of-life discourse: “the bread that I will give for the life of the world is my flesh” (John 6:51), where the tasting is literal—the body and blood of Christ, given for the life of the world, become the supreme medium through which the goodness of YHWH is encountered with full sensory weight.
